E48BCB587708|Four months after Frost's death, Marnette returned to his Archive for the last time.
C447CCA963CD|The journey was more dangerous now than it had been before. The mourning period was ending, and the residence was being prepared for reassignment. Workers came and went, cataloguing Frost's possessions, cleaning the spaces that had accumulated centuries of dust and memory. The historical society had finally begun their assessment of the Archive, sending scholars to document the collection before it was dispersed to various institutions across the colony.
7871958A725B|But Elara had sent word through channels Marnette hadn't known existed—a network of household salves who had learned to communicate without being noticed, passing messages in the margins of supply requisitions and the gaps between shift changes. These were people who had served in Master households for years, watching and listening, learning things they were never supposed to know. The assessment team would be away for three days, attending a conference on colonial history in the administrative district. The residence would be empty except for the minimal maintenance staff who checked in twice daily.
2342820E9EF1|It was now or never.
E94CD391E914|Marnette entered through the service corridor at the hour when shift changes left the passages momentarily unmonitored, using the access code Elara had provided—her last gift before she was reassigned to another Master's household. The residence felt different now, stripped of the personal touches that had made it Frost's home. The medical equipment was gone, the furniture covered in protective sheets, the art removed from the walls and packed for transport. The smell of old books and expensive antiseptic had faded, replaced by the sterile scent of cleaning solutions and the mustiness of rooms that had been sealed for too long.
7C2EFB7B6837|Only the Archive remained untouched, waiting for the scholars to complete their work.
2D40A2F82A49|The door opened at her touch—her clearance still active, still programmed by a dead man who had wanted her to find what he couldn't tell her directly. She wondered, sometimes, if Frost had known how this would unfold. If he had planned for her to return again and again, each time finding more pieces of the truth. Or if he had simply opened a door and hoped she would have the courage to walk through it.
41E0DE744A21|The room swallowed her as it had before, but this time she knew what she was looking for. Not just evidence of what had been done to salves, but understanding of why. Not just the mechanism of control, but its origins. Not just the crime, but the context that had made it seem, to those who committed it, like something other than a crime.
8E6B57CC3AC7|She had shared everything she'd found with the network—forty-one people now, with more joining every week—but questions remained. Questions about how it had started, about what had driven the founders to make such a monstrous decision, about whether the current Masters truly understood what they were perpetuating or whether they too were trapped in a system they hadn't created.
23F758DEBCEC|The answers were here, somewhere in the vast collection Frost had accumulated over nearly eight centuries. She just had to find them before the scholars returned and the Archive was sealed forever.
2F363994DCBF|***
3AFD6FC2F860|She started with the historical records—the oldest documents in the collection, preserved in climate-controlled cases that had kept them intact for seven hundred years. These weren't the bureaucratic proposals and implementation schedules she had found before, but something older. Primary sources. Accounts written by people who had been there at the beginning, when the colony was young and its survival was uncertain.
C1DD1BDFB4EB|The first document was a personal journal, its pages yellowed and fragile despite the preservation technology, its handwriting cramped and difficult to read. The script was an older form, the language slightly different from modern colonial standard—evidence of how much things had changed over seven centuries. The author was identified only as "E.M."—initials that Marnette now recognized with a chill that ran through her entire body.
9CE4D3A5D24D|Elon Musk. The founder himself. The man who had become Elom.
1D848C76F900|She read slowly, carefully, afraid that the pages might crumble if she handled them too roughly, afraid of what she would find and equally afraid of what she might miss. The journal chronicled the final years of the Mars expedition—the desperate scramble to establish a self-sustaining colony as Earth's resource wars intensified, the difficult choices that had to be made when supplies ran low, the gradual realization that they were cut off forever.
C7AB7185BC6E|The early entries were almost mundane—logistics of dome construction, challenges with atmospheric processors, efforts to establish reliable food production. But as the pages turned, the tone shifted. Something was happening on Earth. Something terrible.
426DF0D4230E|"The transmissions from home are growing more disturbing each day," one entry read. "The African Federation has collapsed. Europe is fragmenting into city-states fighting over the last agricultural zones. North America is sealing its borders and shooting anyone who approaches. The oceans are dying—the algae blooms have consumed everything, and the fish populations have crashed below recovery levels. They're saying the food supply will fail completely within a decade."
59F5F5C3C230|A later entry: "We received the final transmission today. Mumbai is gone—flooded when the sea walls failed. São Paulo is gone—burned in the food riots. The North American agricultural zones have failed completely, poisoned by the desperate measures they took to squeeze out a few more harvests. They're fighting over the last scraps down there, and within a generation there won't be anything left to fight over. We are the last hope for humanity. Everything depends on what we build here."
F95EA18BCEF3|The weight of that responsibility—the survival of the entire human species resting on a few thousand colonists on a hostile world—was palpable in every line. Marnette found herself feeling something unexpected: sympathy. Not for what they had done, but for the impossible position they had found themselves in. They had been trying to save humanity. They had been terrified of failing. They had watched their home world destroy itself and known they were the only ones left.
9E7BF6C6E2CF|Another entry, dated several years later, after the last ship from Earth had arrived carrying refugees from the dying planet: "The population projections are unsustainable. We saved as many as we could—the final evacuation brought another twelve thousand, including many of the best scientists and engineers—but now we have too many people for the resources we can produce. At current growth rates, we'll exceed our resource capacity within three generations. The fusion reactors can only produce so much power. The hydroponic farms can only feed so many people. The water recyclers can only process so much waste. We need either population control or massive increases in efficiency, and I don't see how we achieve either without sacrificing something essential about who we are."
247C6DA2C5A8|Marnette turned the pages, watching the founder's desperation grow. Entry after entry detailed failed attempts to solve the resource problem—rationing systems that caused social unrest, birth limitations that were resisted and evaded, technological solutions that fell short of projections. The colony was dying slowly, the same resource crisis that had destroyed Earth now threatening to destroy its last remnant.
5A2D3A4C0981|And then, the entry that changed everything: "Dr. Chen presented her proposal today. Genetic modification of the workforce population to optimize lifespan parameters. It sounds monstrous when you say it like that, and I told her so. But she makes a compelling case, and the math supports her conclusions. If the workers live fifty years instead of eighty, we reduce resource consumption by nearly forty percent while maintaining productive capacity. We could sustain a larger population, ensure the colony's long-term survival, give humanity a real chance at a future. All by accepting that some humans would live shorter lives than others."
938A254175D1|Marnette's hands trembled as she held the journal. This was the moment. This was where it had started—not with malice, but with mathematics. Not with hatred, but with fear. Not with evil, but with desperation.
DB6FCB7E77DD|She kept reading.
F772467C580E|"The debate lasted three days. I've never seen the council so divided. Some argued that we had no right to make this decision, that we were playing god with human lives, that we were creating a permanent underclass and calling it salvation. Others argued that we had no choice, that the alternative was extinction, that future generations would thank us for the hard choice we made to ensure their existence.
72AF5C7FC5A1|"I listened to both sides. I changed my mind a dozen times. I thought about my children, about their children, about the long chain of descendants who might exist if we found a way to survive. And I thought about the workers who would live shorter lives so that chain could continue. I thought about asking them, about giving them a choice. But we couldn't risk them saying no. The math was clear. Without this change, we all die. With it, some live shorter lives but humanity continues.
A57373390818|"What kind of monster chooses extinction over compromise?"
8A5268FC34E0|And then, the vote: "The decision was made this morning. Seven hundred twelve in favor, three hundred eighty-nine opposed. Nearly a third of the council couldn't accept it, couldn't bring themselves to engineer human beings for limited lifespans. I respect their position. Part of me shares it. But the math doesn't care about our moral qualms. We made our choice. Now we have to live with it."
7F1417F8A30B|Seven hundred twelve to three hundred eighty-nine. The same numbers Frost had whispered in his delirium, the vote that had haunted him for centuries. And now Marnette understood why. He hadn't voted for murder. He had voted for survival. He had made an impossible choice under impossible circumstances, and he had spent eight hundred years watching the consequences unfold.
3E09568BC192|***
AB53E9C9350F|The journal continued, chronicling the implementation of what they called the "Sustainability Protocol." The genetic modifications were introduced gradually, over several generations, until the workforce population had been fully transformed. The early entries described the technical challenges—getting the cellular degradation sequences to trigger reliably at the right age, ensuring the modifications were hereditary without unexpected mutations, calibrating the lifespan parameters to maximize efficiency while minimizing suffering.
C6C985A21B69|"We want them to be healthy until the end," one entry noted. "Active, productive, capable of contributing fully until their time comes. And then a quick decline, a gentle death. Not years of suffering like we saw on Earth, where people lived too long and died too slowly, consuming resources they could no longer help produce. This way is kinder. More humane. That's what we tell ourselves."
C1D655619F93|But as the years passed, the tone shifted. The technical discussions gave way to something more troubled, more uncertain. The founder was watching his creation take shape, and he was beginning to doubt.
F9ECC77ECDEA|"The first generation of modified workers has reached maturity," one entry read. "They're healthy, capable, eager to serve the colony. They don't know what was done to them. They think their shorter lifespans are natural, a quirk of Martian biology, an adaptation to the new environment. We tell them nothing, and they don't ask. Is this kindness or cruelty? I can no longer tell."
D9D9AD44DE33|And later, years later, with a trembling hand that suggested age and fatigue: "I watched a worker die today. She was fifty-three, the same age my grandmother was when I was born on Earth. Grandmother lived to ninety-one. This woman—Sara was her name—she just... stopped. One moment she was working, the next she was gone. Cellular breakdown, perfectly on schedule, exactly as designed. I created that. I voted for that. And I watched her daughter weep over her body, thanking Elom for the gift of her mother's years, never knowing that I decided how many years there would be."
21BBF7E8A1C3|Another entry, near the end of the journal: "Dr. Martinez proposed the religious framework today. She says the workers need meaning, need purpose, need something to help them accept their shorter lives without resentment. A manufactured faith, built on the bones of the old religions from Earth, modified to serve our needs. It feels wrong—manipulating their deepest beliefs, their understanding of existence itself—but she's right that we need something. Bare truth would be unbearable. F6894992C29A|The Church of Elom. Manufactured from the beginning. Not organic faith but engineered belief, designed to make salves accept what would otherwise be unacceptable. And the founder himself had approved it, knowing it was manipulation, choosing manipulation over the alternative of truth.
EFD51EEDAE99|And then, the final entry in the journal, dated decades after the first, written in a hand that shook with age and something else—perhaps grief, perhaps guilt: "I'm dying. Not from the modifications—the administrative class was exempted from those—but from simple old age. I've lived a hundred and thirty-seven years, far longer than any human in history before the longevity treatments, and I've watched three generations of workers live and die in the time it took me to grow old. Each death feels like murder. Each death reminds me of the choice we made, the compromise that saved humanity and destroyed something essential about it. I hope the ones who come after us will find a way to undo what we've done. I hope they'll be better than we were. But I fear they'll simply inherit our sins and call them virtues, maintain the system because it benefits them, forget that it was ever a choice at all."
66481175FF8D|Marnette set the journal down and sat in silence, tears streaming down her face. She had come here looking for villains, for monsters who had deliberately chosen to enslave and murder an entire population. What she had found was more complicated, more tragic, more human than she had expected.
2118CF15CA56|They had been afraid. They had been desperate. They had watched their home world die and determined not to let the same happen to their new home. They had made an impossible choice under impossible circumstances, and then they had spent generations living with the consequences, watching the system they had created take on a life of its own.
A2674F6F73D4|It didn't excuse what they had done. Nothing could excuse it. But it explained it. And somehow, that made it worse.
A945C7B18F12|***
37519BDDE9B7|The rest of the Archive filled in the gaps—administrative records, scientific reports, theological documents, correspondence between Masters across the centuries. Marnette read until her eyes burned and her head ached, assembling the complete picture of how the colony had evolved from desperate survival measure to entrenched caste system.
5416C157D6B3|The early generations of Masters had known what they were doing. They had maintained the lie consciously, deliberately, because they believed it was necessary. But as centuries passed, as the original founders died and were replaced by their children and grandchildren, something changed. The knowledge became less explicit, more assumed. C3CBAB44419E|By the time Frost was born—centuries after the founding—the truth had become obscure even to most Masters. They inherited a system and assumed it was the way things had always been, the way things were meant to be.
2EA482687A49|But some Masters knew. Some had access to the historical records, to the founding documents, to the truth that had been carefully preserved even as it was hidden from the general population. Frost had been one of them. B3C35E6350E2|And he hadn't been alone. A document dated from a century before Frost's death recorded a secret meeting of Masters who had discovered the truth. Twelve of them, gathering in private to discuss what they had learned. F82A06BDF92E|The debate had been fierce, the document showed. Some had argued for full disclosure, for truth and reconciliation, for treating salves as equals and working together to build a better colony. "We have the technology now to extend salve lifespans," one had written. "We have the resources to support a larger population. The desperate circumstances that created this system no longer exist. 5EECDDAD9A7B|Others had argued that the truth would be too destabilizing, that centuries of accumulated resentment would erupt into violence, that the colony couldn't survive the upheaval that would follow. 4FF71BAC0F65|In the end, they had chosen silence. Not because they were evil, but because they were afraid. Afraid of what the truth would do to a society built on lies. Afraid that the cure would be worse than the disease. Afraid that honesty, after so many centuries of deception, was simply impossible.
4D22651C4C46|And so the lie had continued, generation after generation, maintained not by conspiracy but by inertia. 5ECFBB47F830|***
150AF8571439|The document that hurt the most was the one she found last—a proposal from just fifty years ago, during the Great Optimization that Marcus remembered. A proposal to "refine" the lifespan parameters, to tighten the range of variation, to make the system more efficient and predictable.
B93DB6581F1F|The reasoning was coldly practical, written in the bloodless language of bureaucratic efficiency. Some salves were living too long, accumulating too much knowledge, asking too many questions. The variations in lifespan were creating instability—families where one sibling died at forty-five while another lived to sixty, communities where elders remembered things they shouldn't remember, individuals who lived long enough to start connecting dots that should have remained unconnected.
957B41AF65C3|"The current parameter range of 45-70 years introduces unacceptable variance," the proposal stated. 61C7401BC2BD|The Optimization was designed to eliminate outliers like Marcus's grandmother, who had lived to sixty-eight and remembered when the prayers were different. F573C11F4BB6|It wasn't the founding Masters who had ordered this. 66F10D89FFCF|The vote had been 847 to 312. An even larger majority than the original decision, made by people who had never faced the desperate circumstances that created the system, who had no excuse except convenience and self-interest.
A2332962B960|This was what Marnette had needed to understand. The founding had been tragedy—desperate people making desperate choices. But the Optimization had been something else. It had been current Masters, living Masters, making a conscious decision to continue the oppression. Not because they had to, not because survival demanded it, but because it was convenient. Because it was easier than change. Because they had grown comfortable with a system that served their interests at the expense of salve lives.
81F2D7EB04B8|The founding could be forgiven, perhaps, as the product of impossible circumstances. But the Optimization? The active choice to tighten the chains, to shorten the lives, to prevent the questions that might lead to truth?
EE12FEC86E58|That was something else entirely.
87E4F5A97E47|***
41BB53815D2C|Marnette gathered the most important documents—copies she had made during her hours in the Archive, careful reproductions that captured the essence of what she had learned. The original journal of Elon Musk, with its tormented entries about the choice he had made. The records of the founding debate, with its final vote of 712 to 389. The secret meeting of conscience-stricken Masters who had discovered the truth and chosen silence. The correspondence between those who knew, wrestling with their complicity across generations. And the proposal for the Optimization, with its cold logic and its damning vote of 847 to 312.
5502FC876534|This was the complete picture now. Not just evidence of what had been done, but understanding of why. 86F5F3D7B2FF|She had come looking for monsters. She had found human beings—flawed, afraid, capable of both great evil and genuine regret. She had found a tragedy that had become a system, a desperate choice that had become an institution, a compromise that had become a crime perpetuated across generations.
5472F4C86415|And she had found something else too: proof that the current Masters were not innocent. They might not have created the system, but they maintained it. They might not have voted for the original genetic modifications, but they had voted for the Optimization. 9D1D321E48E3|That knowledge was both terrible and necessary. Because it meant that change was possible. If the system was maintained by choice, then different choices could unmaintain it. If the current order was the result of decisions, then new decisions could create a new order.
34E78A20C146|***
ABBAB9104E1C|She left the Archive as the artificial dawn began to brighten outside, the documents hidden beneath her tunic, her mind full of everything she had learned. This was the last time she would enter this room. The scholars would return, the collection would be dispersed, and the evidence Frost had preserved would be scattered across the colony—some of it destroyed, some of it buried in institutions where no one would think to look for it.
DB4C09F965C8|But Marnette had what she needed. The truth, complete at last. The full story, from beginning to present. The knowledge she would share with the network, helping them understand not just what they were fighting against but why it had come to exist.
68C485A2E674|The journey back to the salve district felt different now—longer, heavier, freighted with the weight of seven hundred years of history she now understood in ways she hadn't before. The other salves she passed in the corridors looked different to her now. Not just victims of a crime, but inheritors of a tragedy. Their ancestors had been created in desperation, modified and manipulated by people who were trying to save humanity from extinction. Their faith had been manufactured by people who genuinely believed they were providing comfort, not control. Their shorter lives were the result of calculations that had weighed survival against individual liberty and chosen survival.
8F41DB137F3F|None of this made it right. None of this excused what had been done or what continued to be done. But it complicated the simple narrative of evil Masters and innocent salves, of villains and victims, of oppressors who deserved nothing but hatred.
1C408464808C|The founders had been human. Afraid, flawed, desperate to survive. They had made a terrible choice and spent generations living with the consequences.
73889313D488|The current Masters were also human. Inheritors of a system they hadn't created, maintainers of an order they had been taught was natural and necessary. Some of them knew the truth and chose to perpetuate the lie. Others lived in ignorance as complete as any salve's.
15F93D43039C|And the salves themselves were human. D2F9929FDD67|This was what Marnette would share with the network. Not just evidence of crimes, but understanding of systems. Not just rage at oppressors, but recognition of the humanity that existed on all sides. Not just the fire of righteous anger, but the more difficult work of building something better from the ashes of what had been destroyed.
232590CC327F|***
C45E74DF5453|The network met that night—as many as could gather safely, seven people in a cramped storage room in the agricultural section. Marnette spread the documents across the floor and told them what she had learned.
1CC65811F401|She told them about Elon Musk, the founder who had become their god, his private journals revealing a man tormented by the choices he had made. She told them about the vote—seven hundred twelve to three hundred eighty-nine—and the desperate logic that had driven it. She told them about the Church, manufactured alongside the genetic modifications to make acceptance possible. She told them about the generations of Masters who had inherited the system and maintained it, some knowingly and others in ignorance.
4C0E98E6CA78|And she told them about the Optimization, just fifty years ago, when current Masters had chosen to tighten the chains—not because survival demanded it, but because they could, because it was efficient, because they had grown comfortable with a system that served their interests.
02E009A15958|The reactions were mixed, faces showing confusion and anger and something harder to name.
48FA5FA5D53C|"So we're not supposed to hate them?" Thomas asked, his young voice sharp with frustration. "We're supposed to understand why they murdered our families, why they stole our lives, why they lied to us for seven hundred years?"
C576D0062CBB|"We can hate what they did," Marnette said carefully. "We should hate what they did. But understanding why they did it helps us figure out how to change it. 5F4250D8AA9D|"Some of them," Vera said thoughtfully, her organizational mind working through the implications. "The ones who don't know the full truth, who inherited the system without understanding it. But the ones who do know, the ones who voted for the Optimization—"
7C2EA3B8BD2D|"They chose to perpetuate it. They're guilty of that choice." Marnette nodded. "But even they are human. Even they can change. Frost changed. BE597E69C8CD|"One Master out of how many?" Jonas's voice was bitter with decades of accumulated doubt. "One conscience out of thousands? That's not a movement. That's an anomaly."
1855FF88A496|"One is how it starts," Petra said quietly. "One person who sees differently, who chooses differently. That's how we started too. F378546F95BA|The discussion continued late into the night, wrestling with the implications of everything Marnette had learned. A7278479253A|"If we only tell them about the Optimization, they'll hate the Masters who voted for it," Chen argued, his engineer's mind seeking precision. "But they might think the original decision was justified. They might accept the system while condemning its recent modifications."
2013011027BA|"And if we tell them the whole story, they might sympathize too much," Thomas countered. "They might forgive the unforgiven, accept the unacceptable. The founders made their choice. They don't deserve our understanding."
14D16308987E|"Understanding isn't forgiveness," Marnette said. "You can understand why someone did something terrible and still condemn them for doing it. 069C2102CC2F|When they finally dispersed, each carrying copies of the new documents, they carried something else too: a fuller understanding of what they were fighting against and what they were fighting for.
8930EB53D8E0|Not revenge. Not the destruction of the Masters as a class. But transformation. B1D88A86FFAA|It was a harder goal than simple revolution. It required not just anger but empathy, not just courage but patience, not just the willingness to fight but the willingness to build.
64EE0EE58A12|But it was the only goal that offered a future worth living in.
613C40A36D95|***
050E7E333E98|Marnette walked home through the gray corridors as the artificial night settled over the district. The documents were hidden safely, distributed among the network, their contents already being shared in whispered conversations across the colony.
18529907EED1|The truth was out now. Not just evidence of crimes, but understanding of systems. Not just the what but the why. And with understanding came the possibility of something she had barely dared to hope for: change that didn't require destroying everything, but rather transforming it.
EA91FE8A2972|She thought of Frost, dying in his bed, desperate to confess. He had known all of this. 95DE477F5570|"I understand now," she whispered to his memory as she walked. "I understand why you couldn't just tell me. The truth was too big for words, too complicated for a deathbed confession. I had to find it myself, piece by piece, until I could hold it all in my mind."
4D5C3E7BEE18|She reached her quarters and paused at the door, looking at the small space that had been her home for years. The carving of Elom still faced the wall. The prayer beads still lay on her bedside table, untouched since the day she had stopped believing.
2232AFE1460E|Tomorrow she would share what she had learned with more people. Tomorrow the network would grow, the truth would spread, the cracks in the foundation would multiply.
E234E22C11D3|But tonight, she allowed herself a moment of something like peace. Not the false peace of faith, not the comfortable peace of ignorance, but something harder and more honest. 17ED1147F619|The truth of Elom was that there was no Elom. Just human beings, making choices, building systems, perpetuating lies that grew larger than themselves.
402797DEAE69|But if humans had built this system, humans could change it. If choices had created this order, different choices could create a different order.
6EB80171F942|That was the truth Marnette carried now. That was the seed Frost had planted, finally bearing fruit.
9FDD3201B9D0|And tomorrow, she would plant seeds of her own.
